name: Codebase
question: How is your application's source code organised across repositories?
hint: One app = one repo. Multiple environments (dev, staging, prod) should be deployments of that same codebase, not separate branches or repos.

options.text: One repo, deployed to dev/staging/prod using environment-specific config or tags
One repo, but we manually copy or cherry-pick code between environment branches
Separate repos per environment (e.g. a prod repo and a dev repo)
No version control - code is edited directly on the server

name: Dependencies
question: How are your app's third-party dependencies managed?
hint: All dependencies should be explicitly declared (e.g. package.json, requirements.txt, go.mod) and isolated so the app never relies on system-wide packages.

options.text: Declared in a manifest with a lock file, installed into an isolated environment (virtualenv, node_modules, container)
Declared in a manifest but no lock file - versions may drift between installs
Some dependencies are installed globally on the server and not declared anywhere
No formal dependency management - libraries are committed directly into the repo

name: Config
question: Where does your app read its configuration from (API keys, database URLs, feature flags, ports)?
hint: Config that varies between environments must not be hardcoded or committed to source control. Environment variables or a secrets manager are the standard approaches.

options.text: Environment variables or a secrets manager (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Doppler, etc.)
Config files that are not committed to the repo (e.g. .env files deployed manually)
Config files committed to the repo, with different files per environment
Hardcoded values directly in the source code

name: Backing services
question: How does your app connect to external services like databases, caches, queues, or third-party APIs?
hint: Backing services should be attached via a URL or connection string stored in config - swapping a local database for a managed cloud one should require no code changes.

options.text: Via connection strings or URLs in env vars - swapping providers requires no code change
Via config files that vary per environment, but still requires a redeploy to switch
Connection details are hardcoded - switching providers requires code changes
Services run on the same machine as the app and are accessed via localhost or socket

name: Build, release, run
question: How does code go from a merged pull request to running in production?
hint: The build (compile/package), release (combine with config), and run (execute) stages should be strictly separate and automated. A release should be immutable - never patch code directly on a running server.

options.text: CI/CD pipeline builds an artifact (Docker image, binary, etc.), combines it with config, then deploys it - production is never patched directly
Automated pipeline exists but config is baked into the build, so a config change requires a full rebuild
Deployment is semi-manual (e.g. running a script or clicking deploy), and hotfixes are sometimes applied directly on the server
Code is pushed or SSH'd onto the server directly, with no formal build step

name: Processes
question: How does your app handle data that needs to persist between requests (sessions, uploads, in-memory state)?
hint: App processes should be stateless. Anything that needs to persist - sessions, files, queued jobs - should live in a backing service (Redis, S3, a database), not in the process's memory or local disk.

options.text: Processes are stateless - sessions use a shared store (Redis, DB), and uploaded files go to object storage (S3, GCS)
Mostly stateless, but some features use sticky sessions or local temp files that are not critical
Sessions or file uploads are stored locally on the server - this breaks if a second instance is added
The app is a single long-running instance that holds state in memory

name: Port binding
question: How is your application served - what handles incoming HTTP requests?
hint: The app should be self-contained and bind to a port itself (via an embedded server like Express, Gunicorn, or a container). It should not depend on an external web server injecting a runtime.

options.text: The app starts its own HTTP server and binds to a port (Express, Gunicorn, Spring Boot, etc.), optionally behind a reverse proxy
The app is deployed as a serverless function (Lambda, Cloud Run) - port binding is handled by the platform
The app is a module loaded by Apache or Nginx (mod_wsgi, mod_php) - it cannot run independently
Not sure - the server setup is managed by someone else and we do not have visibility into it

name: Concurrency
question: How do you handle increased traffic or load on your application?
hint: Scale by running more identical, stateless process instances (horizontal scaling) rather than making a single instance bigger. The process model should map different workloads to different process types (web, worker, scheduler).

options.text: We scale horizontally by adding more instances (containers, pods, dynos) - the app is stateless so any instance can handle any request
We use a mix - some horizontal scaling, but parts of the app are stateful and need manual handling during scale-up
We scale vertically by upgrading the server (more CPU/RAM) - we run a single instance
Scaling has not been considered - we deal with it when it becomes a problem

name: Disposability
question: What happens when a new version of your app is deployed or an instance crashes?
hint: Instances should start quickly (under a few seconds), shut down gracefully (finishing in-flight requests, not dropping connections), and be safely restartable at any time.

options.text: Instances start in seconds, handle SIGTERM by finishing current requests before exiting, and use health checks so traffic only routes to ready instances
Startup is fast but shutdown is abrupt - in-flight requests may be dropped during deployments
Startup takes a long time (minutes) or requires manual warm-up steps before the instance is ready
We do not have rolling deployments - traffic is interrupted when we deploy

name: Dev/prod parity
question: How closely does your local development environment match production?
hint: Differences between dev and prod (OS, runtime versions, services) are a leading cause of 'works on my machine' bugs. Containers and tools like Docker Compose help keep environments in sync.

options.text: Dev uses the same Docker images and service versions as prod - differences are minimal and intentional
Same services are used but versions may differ, or the OS differs between dev (Mac/Windows) and prod (Linux)
Dev uses lightweight substitutes (SQLite instead of Postgres, mocked APIs) that behave differently from prod services
Dev is a local setup with no relation to prod - bugs that only appear in prod are common

name: Logs
question: How does your app produce and manage logs?
hint: The app should write logs as a stream to stdout/stderr and nothing else. Routing, storing, and alerting on logs is the infrastructure's responsibility (Datadog, CloudWatch, ELK, etc.).

options.text: The app writes structured logs (JSON) to stdout/stderr - the platform routes them to a centralised log aggregator
The app writes plain-text logs to stdout, which are collected by an agent or the platform
The app writes to log files on disk and manages its own log rotation
Logging is inconsistent - some things are printed, others are silent, and there is no central place to query logs

name: Admin processes
question: How do you run one-off tasks like database migrations, data backups, or maintenance scripts?
hint: Admin tasks should run in the same environment and from the same codebase as the app, not from a developer's local machine or a separate unversioned script.

options.text: Tasks run as part of the CI/CD pipeline or as one-off container jobs (kubectl run, Fargate task, Heroku run) using the same image as the app
Tasks are scripts in the repo but run manually by SSH-ing into a server
Tasks are run from a developer's local machine against the production database
No standard process - each task is handled differently and not documented
